rationale: The citing paper directly manipulates incidental social vs. nonsocial background music across experiments and states it "extends work on incidental music effects" by proposing a mechanism based on cue–product applicability and self-focused attention. The cited 2005 JMR paper is specifically about how background music meanings affect product perceptions, making it a plausible theory/mechanism source rather than an incidental mention (with an exact DOI match in the references).
